coredata.dc:title: Achieving steam and electrical power from solar energy by MoS2-based composites
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Zhenzhen
Wang, Jun
Wang, Yifei
Wang, Jianying
Li, Jinhua
Mei, Tao
Qian, Jingwen
Wang, Xianbao
coredata.dc:description: Photothermal conversion has hold great promise for facing water scarcity by solar evaporation technology. However, the photothermal performance of the evaporators is limited by weather rhythm due to strong dependence on solar energy. Herein, MoS2-based composites are well-suited for maximize resource utilization by achieving steam and electrical power from solar energy. Under one sun radiation, the output voltage can be up to ~ 300 mV synchronously with efficient solar evaporation. The power output is ~ 3.35 mW m−2 with current of 6.7 µA after half an hour of one sun illumination. The power output of ~ 0.9 mW m−2 can be readily derived with voltage of ~ 200 mV and current of 2.7 µA under ambient conditions. Furthermore, the voltage output reveals a linear increase behavior through the series connection of energy generators. MoS2-based composites show the potential of simultaneous steam and energy generation under sunlight, and it realizes the value as energy generators on cloudy days or at night. The work demonstrates that MoS2-based composites are capable of full-time function only relying on solar energy and water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Full-time function
MoS2-based composites
Water-driven energy generation
Solar evaporation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of MoS2-based solar evaporators
MoS2-based composites for water-driven energy generation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: MoS2-based composites as efficient solar evaporators and energy generators for solar water evaporation and water-driven energy generation.

(a) The synthetic process of MoS2 by one-step hydrothermal method. (b) SEM image of MoS2. (c) TEM image of MoS2. (d) Raman spectra of MoS2. (e) XRD patterns of MoS2. (f, g) The XPS measurement of Mo 3d spectra and S 2p spectra.
(a) The prepared process of CDs. (b)The Raman spectrum of CDs. (c) FTIR spectrum of the CDs. (d, e) XPS spectra corresponding to the C1s and N1s peaks.
(a, b) The prepared process of MoS2@CDs-SA. (c, d) SEM images of MoS2-SA. (e, f) SEM images of MoS2@CDs-SA. (g, h) Light absorption capabilities of the MoS2-SA and MoS2@CDs-SA.

(a) Schematic of solar water evaporation with MoS2@CDs-SA. (b) The weight change of water with different photothermal materials including water, SA, CDs-SA, MoS2-SA and MoS2@CDs-SA under one sun. (c, d) The time-dependent surface temperature of different samples under one sun. (e) Long-term evaporation cycle test for MoS2@CDs-SA in 3.5 wt% salt water. (d) Long-term evaporation cycle test for MoS2@CDs-SA in high-concentration salt water (20 wt%).

(a) Water-driven energy generation by MoS2-based composites in water environment under ambient conditions. (b) The output voltage of airlaid paper, MoS2-airlaid paper and uneven MoS2-SA in response to water under ambient conditions.
The possible mechanism of water-driven energy generation with MoS2-based composites.

(a) Water-driven energy generation by MoS2-airlaid paper and MoS2@CDs-airlaid paper under ambient conditions. (b) The current and voltage after stabilization (b1 for MoS2, b2 for MoS2@CDs) under ambient conditions. (c) Water-driven energy generation by MoS2-airlaid paper by connecting several devices in series (one device, two devices, three devices and four devices) under ambient conditions.

(a) Water-driven energy generation and solar evaporation by MoS2-airlaid paper under one sun. (b) Comparison of the voltage output with MoS2-airlaid paper under one sun and ambient conditions. (c) The voltage output with one device under light on and light off. (d) The voltage output with two devices under no light and light on. (e) The current after half an hour of one sun illumination and light off for 2 min. (f) Long-term output measurement over 5 h with two devices under ambient conditions. (g) The performance of solar evaporation and energy generation at the same time. (h, i) Charging test using a capacitor of 100 µF.

(a) The performance of solar evaporation and energy generation of MoS2-airlaid paper and MoS2@CDs-airlaid paper at the same time. (b) The current of MoS2@CDs-airlaid paper after half an hour of one sun illumination and light off for 2 min.

head.ce:title: Achieving steam and electrical power from solar energy by MoS2-based composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-constructed by the Province and Ministry, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials
Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials
Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-constructed by the Province and Ministry
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-constructed by the Province and Ministry, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen College of International Education, Shenzhen 518040, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenzhen College of International Education, Shenzhen 518040, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photothermal conversion has hold great promise for facing water scarcity by solar evaporation technology. However, the photothermal performance of the evaporators is limited by weather rhythm due to strong dependence on solar energy. Herein, MoS2-based composites are well-suited for maximize resource utilization by achieving steam and electrical power from solar energy. Under one sun radiation, the output voltage can be up to ~ 300 mV synchronously with efficient solar evaporation. The power output is ~ 3.35 mW m−2 with current of 6.7 µA after half an hour of one sun illumination. The power output of ~ 0.9 mW m−2 can be readily derived with voltage of ~ 200 mV and current of 2.7 µA under ambient conditions. Furthermore, the voltage output reveals a linear increase behavior through the series connection of energy generators. MoS2-based composites show the potential of simultaneous steam and energy generation under sunlight, and it realizes the value as energy generators on cloudy days or at night. The work demonstrates that MoS2-based composites are capable of full-time function only relying on solar energy and water.

ce:section.ce:para: Contrary to abundant seawater, the ever-growing shortage of potable water has long been one of critical challenges in the world, especially in water-poor areas.[1–3] In the past few decades, massive efforts have been committed to seawater desalination research, and recent advances in solar evaporators, which can effectively capture solar energy and convert to thermal, making solar evaporation technology one of most appealing technologies to alleviate freshwater crisis.[4–6] Among, interface-based solar evaporation technology plays a tremendous role in addressing the challenge by allowing efficient steam generation and desalination.[7–9] Exploring high-efficiency solar evaporators and designing desalination systems have been enduring themes of interface-based evaporation technology. Significant progress of structure designs has led to high evaporation efficiency (>90%) combining with photothermal materials.[10,11] In a typically interface-based evaporation system, water evaporation takes place at a confined high-temperature zone, where the solar evaporator of water surface converts solar energy to thermal, and water is continuously pumped up to the high-temperature zone by water channels. The heat loss from the solar evaporator to the bulk water is reduced by a thermal barrier, significantly increasing the evaporation rate. Besides, porous structure designs or hydrophilic/hydrophobic regulations are used to deal with salt blocking problems [12–14].

Because sunlight is an indispensable factor in solar-assisted evaporation technology, most photothermal devices will set aside when there is no light. Several alternative approaches have been proposed lately to face unused resources, such as water capture from humid air[15–17] and water-driven energy generation.[18–21] Water-driven energy generation, as a new kind of green energy, has attracted attention of some researchers. In past few years, Qu’s group has made great progress in water-driven power generation, where the functional materials are mainly concentrated on carbon materials.[22–25] In this perspective, such a green, pollution-free energy generation technology, it is important to develop functional versatility by advanced and lost-cost materials to match up with energy demands. Zhang’s group once designed systems for both solar steam and water-driven energy generation, which provides a basis for multi-functional development. However, the evaporator and the energy accumulator are two separate materials, and the design is complicated.[20,21] It has been noted that 1 T MoS2, as a photothermal material, achieves high-efficiency photothermal conversion, and has been used as evaporator for steam generation in our previous works.[26–28] In addition, with advantages of intrinsic high electrical conductivity,[29] it could be an attractive electrode material. An open-circuit voltage output of 19 mV can be provided by a MoS2 film under moisture.[18] By combining the thermal and electrical properties of MoS2, it is expected to maximize the use of resources.

In this work, we have explored MoS2-based composites as efficient solar evaporators and energy generators for solar steam and water-driven energy generation. In solar steam generation, a porous system (MoS2@CDs-SA) is designed by the ingenious integration of MoS2, CDs with SA, which inherits both the desirable properties and structural merits of the respective building blocks. Specifically, the porous system enhances light capture over the full solar spectrum by adding CDs and the interconnected porous structure supplies adequate water for continuous solar evaporation by capillary pumping capability. Ultimately, the full-spectrum responsive porous system demonstrates a high evaporation rate of 1.9 kg m−2h−1 under one sun. Under dark or cloudy days, MoS2-based composites are well-suited for water-driven energy generation. The power output of ~ 0.9 mW m−2 with voltage of ~ 200 mV can be detected by a MoS2-based energy generator. By one sun radiation, the output power can be further strengthened to ~ 3.35 mW m−2 synchronously with efficient solar evaporation. Furthermore, a voltage output of ~ 800 mV can be achieved with the series connection of four energy generators under ambient conditions. Through the series connection of a series of energy generators, it is expected to realize large-scale energy generation. The result demonstrates that the MoS2-based composites are capable of full-time work only relying on solar energy and water (Fig. 1 ), suggesting a promising avenue to maximize resource utilization for multifunctional applications.

Thioacetamide (C2H5NS), ammonium molybdate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O), p-phenylenediamine (PPD, 99.0%), glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH), ethanol (CH3OH, 99.7%) and calcium carbonate (CaCO3) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (China). Lignin and sodium alginate (SA) were supplied from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

According to our previous report, [26] 0.57 mmol (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O and 8 mmol C2H5NS were mixed to deionized (DI) water (40 mL) under stirring. After one hour, it was transferred to an autoclave (50 mL). Then the mixed solution was heated at 200 °C for 20 h. The product was centrifuged with ethanol, DI water for four times (10000 rpm, 10 min) after cooling to room temperature. Finally, it was dried at 60 °C by a vacuum drying oven.

CDs was synthesized according to our previous method.[30] After PPD (30 mg) and lignin (10 mg) were added to 10 mL ethanol under stirring magnetically for 30 mins, the mixtures were transferred into a 50 mL autoclave. Subsequently, a heating process was carried out at 200 °C for 10 h. Finally, the sample was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove lager precipitates.

MoS2 (50 mg) and CDs (1 mL) were ultrasonically dispersed in DI water (30 mL), and then CaCO3 (1.7 wt%) and SA (1.7 wt%) were added to above solution under magnetic stirring. After mixing well, the solution was poured into a glass container and was frozen vertically for 12 h. And then, it was dried for 48 h by the freezing-dryer. Subsequently, it was soaked in CH3COOH solution (1 mL CH3COOH, 20 mL DI) for 30 min, and then washed for several times in DI water. The gel was performed the vertical freezing and freeze-drying process again. The same process was carried out to synthesize MoS2-SA and CDs-SA.

Uneven MoS2-SA was developed by the same method with MoS2-SA, except that the mixed solution was stand for 6 h before freezing. MoS2-airlaid paper was synthesized by solution loading method. MoS2 (50 mg) was ultrasonically dispersed in 20 mL DI water. The airlaid paper was placed vertically in above solution to make the lower part darker and the upper part lighter, and then dried at 60 °C. After repeating the process twice, the MoS2-based energy generator was successfully obtained. MoS2@CDs-airlaid paper was prepared by the same process, except that CDs (1 mL) was added in the ultrasonic dispersion process.

The morphologies of MoS2, MoS2-SA and MoS2@CDs-SA were observed by Field-emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM JSM7100F) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM JEM-2100F). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement of MoS2 was performed by a D8-advance diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The Raman analysis of MoS2 and CDs were carried out on a LabRAM HR 800UV (HORIBA JobinYvon, France) (532 nm excitation laser). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments of MoS2 and CDs were conducted on THERMO FISHERSICENTIFIC Escalab 250Xi. The light performance measurements of MoS2-SA and MoS2@CDs-SA were recorded by a Shimadze UV–VIS-NIR UV-3600 double beam spectrophotometer. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of CDs was obtained from FT-IR tester (NICOLET iS50, USA).

Solar-assisted evaporation tests were carried out by a solar simulator (PLS-FX300HU) with a solar filter (AM1.5). The weight changes of water evaporated with different samples were recorded by a computer connected an electronic balance. The light intensity was kept at one sun (1 kW m−2) by a full-spectrum optical power meter (CEL-NP2000-2, Beijing Education Au-light Co., Ltd.). The data of surface temperature were measured by infrared camera (FLIR E4 Pro, America) and thermocouples (OMEGA, INSP# 33306, 5TC-TT-K-30–197), and were recorded by a data acquisition system (KEYSIGHT, 34972A). The entire solar evaporation experiment was tested at a room temperature of 26 ~ 28 °C and a humidity of 50 ~ 55%.

A piece of energy generator (2*3 cm−2) was clamped on plexiglass through conductive alligator clips, and then placing it in water surface. Electrical property data were mainly performed by a data acquisition system (KEYSIGHT, 34972A) and a multimeter. A series of energy generators were interconnected by copper tape to scale up the overall energy output.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of MoS2-based solar evaporators
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic process of MoS2 is schematically illustrated in Fig. 2 a, in which one-step hydrothermal process was carried out with (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O and C2H5NS as raw materials. The MoS2 displays a flower-like structure with nano-sized flower pieces about 20 ~ 30 nm (Fig. 2b). TEM image shows that MoS2 synthesized possesses a large interlayer spacing of 0.91 nm, which suggests that the MoS2 contains 1 T phase (Fig. 2c).[31,32] The generation of 1 T phase was further demonstrated by Raman, XRD and XPS characterization. Raman spectra of the MoS2 (Fig. 2d) shows two typical peaks at 282.4 and 376.8 cm−1 corresponding to the E1g and E 1 2g of 2H MoS2. The additional peaks of 1 T phase could be found at 194.3, 236.7, 335.8 cm−1. A low angle diffraction peak of 2θ ≈ 9.8° is shown in XRD pattern (Fig. 2e), revealing an interlayer spacing of 0.91 nm calculated by the Bragg equation. The XPS measurements (Fig. 2f and g) reveal two characteristic peaks of Mo 3d spectra at about 229.5 and 232.8 eV corresponding to Mo 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 of 2H phase and two additional peaks around 228.7 and 231.9 eV of 1 T phase. Similarly, the peaks of S 2p spectra also show two known S 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 peaks at around 161.9 and 163.6 eV of 2H phase and two additional peaks at 161.5 and 162.8 eV. These results further prove the generation of dual-phase MoS2. It has been proved that MoS2 containing 1 T phase possesses better application prospects than 2H MoS2 contributing to better photothermal performance and higher light absorption.[26,33]

CDs have been reported as light-capturing materials for high-efficiency solar evaporation.[34–36] It is based on two reasons: i) CDs show a densely packed π electron energy level with small size and can absorb a wide range of solar spectrum, thereby exciting electrons and relaxing to the ground state to generate heat;[37] ii) CDs have a strong ability to absorb short-wavelength light, under mixing with other substances, the light will be further converted into heat by non-radiative.[35,38] Combining the advantages of MoS2 and CDs, it is very possible to achieve stronger light absorption and thus increase the evaporation rate. CDs were synthesized by one-step solvothermal method with lignin as starting material (Fig. 3 a). As a natural aromatic bio-structure polymer, lignin can be easily converted to graphitic carbon by a mild pyrolysis.[34] As shown of Raman spectrum in Fig. 3b, lignin is successfully converted to graphitic carbon with peaks at 1343 and 1572 cm−1 relating to the D and G band of graphitic carbon, respectively. TEM measurements show the morphologies and sizes of CDs (Figure S1). The CDs show 0.21 nm of well-resolved lattice fringes, which is related to the (100) in-plane lattice spacing of graphene. FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3c) showed that the peaks at 3365, 1658, 1520, 1383 and 1048 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of N–H/–OH groups, C = N stretching peak, C = C stretching peak, C-N stretching vibration and phenolic C-O stretching vibrations, respectively. The XPS spectra (Fig. 3d) were further tested to identify the chemical structure of CDs. C1s spectrum reveals four characteristic peaks at about 283.7, 284.3, 286.2 and 287.4 eV corresponding to C–C/C = C, C-O, C-N and C-OH. Besides, the N1s spectrum (Fig. 3e) shows the present of pyridinic N (397.7 eV), amino N (398.1 eV) and pyrrolic N (399.2 eV).

Integrating good light absorption, continuous water supply, low thermal conductivity, floatability and effective salt ion exchange performance, porous evaporation systems have always been one of the research hotspots of solar evaporation technology, including aerogel,[39,40] hydrogel,[41–43] and polymer foam.[44–46] In the work, SA gel is used as water supply framework to combine with photothermal materials for effective photothermal evaporation. As an edible adhesive, SA exhibits environmental friendliness and it can easily cross-link with Ca2+ ions to form a water-insoluble porous gel-like structure.[47] Fig. 4 a illustrates the synthesis process of the MoS2-based porous system. MoS2, CDs as photothermal materials, and CaCO3 as a source of Ca2+ ions were mixed in SA solution and freeze-dried vertically to shape the initial porous structure. Subsequently, the porous system was put into CH3COOH solution, in which CH3COOH reacted with CaCO3 to release Ca2+, thereby achieving a stereotype of water-insoluble porous gel-like structure (Fig. 4b). SEM was employed to confirm topography and microstructure of MoS2-based porous system. Both MoS2-SA (Fig. 4c and d) and MoS2@CDs-SA (Fig. 4e and f) show abundant pores and cells with the size of 50–300 μm. The adjacent cells clearly connect to form well interpenetrate low-curvature pore channels, which is more conducive to water transfer and salt ion exchange.[12,44] The light absorption capabilities of the MoS2-SA and MoS2@CDs-SA were investigated from the range 300–2000 nm. As shown in Fig. 4g and h, After the addition of CDs, the resulting MoS2@CDs-SA exhibits higher light absorption than MoS2-SA with almost 95% absorption, indicating that the CDs can enhance the light capture ability. With such excellent light absorption performance, MoS2@CDs-SA can serve as a desirable absorber for capturing solar energy to achieve efficient solar water evaporation.

With the merits of excellent light absorption and highly porous structure, MoS2@CDs-SA can be regarded as an ideal candidate to alleviate freshwater crisis by solar water evaporation technology. The evaporation performance of MoS2@CDs-SA was systematically investigated under one sun by utilizing a real-time measurement system (Fig. 5 a). As shown in Fig. 5b, the weight goes down linearly with increasing irradiation time. It clearly reveals that the MoS2@CDs-SA has more water evaporated per unit area than the contrasted evaporators. The evaporation rates of different sample were calculated from the slope of the mass change curves (Figure S2). After half an hour of one sun radiation, for water, SA and CDs-SA, the evaporation rates were stabilized at 0.234, 0.41 and 0.82 kg m-2h−1, respectively. The evaporation rate of MoS2-SA can be obviously stabilized to 1.52 kg m-2h−1, which is 6.5 times that of water under the same condition. The MoS2@CDs-SA displays the highest stable evaporation rate up to 1.9 kg m-2h−1 (Calculation S1 for the energy loss in evaporation process and Figure S3 for the environment temperature upward the absorber under one sun). The enhancement of evaporation performance should be attributed to the introduction of CDs: i) As a carbon-based photothermal material, CDs have inherent light properties and enhance light absorption; ii) With small size, CDs possess a densely packed π electron energy level, thus exciting electrons and relaxing to the ground state to generate heat under sun radiation; iii) CDs have a strong ability to absorb short-wavelength light, under mixing with other substances, the light may be further converted into heat by non-radiative (Figure S4). The surface temperatures of MoS2-SA and MoS2@CDs-SA can rapidly increase to 32.5 and 33.2 °C within 5 min (Fig. 5c and d), while the temperature of SA only increases into ~ 29.3 °C for 30 min, evidencing the rapid light response of the MoS2-SA and MoS2@CDs-SA. After 30 cycles of testing, the MoS2@CDs-SA still maintains good photothermal stability with a stable evaporation rate (Figure S5). We also detected the photothermal stability of MoS2@CDs-SA in salt water by a long-term cycle test. As shown in Fig. 5e, after four consecutive cycles in 3.5 wt% salt water (two hours each time), the MoS2@CDs-SA can still maintain good integrity and efficient solar evaporation performance. Subsequently, the same sample was put into a high-concentration salt water (20 wt%) again for further stability test (Fig. 5f). After four hours of continuous work, it can still guarantee a stable evaporation rate of ~ 1.9 kg m-2h−1, and there were almost no salt crystals on the surface (Figure S6), which should contribute to the low-curvature pore structure for effective salt ion exchange. In addition, MoS2@CDs-SA demonstrates a good desalination performance. As shown in Figure S7, the concentrations of the ions (Na+, Mg2+, K+, and Ca2+) decrease significantly after desalination, which is much lower than the required standard of drinking water (Chinese National Standard GB5749-2006, World Health Organization).

ce:section.ce:section-title: MoS2-based composites for water-driven energy generation
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to its clean, abundant, and easily exploitable attributes, the water-driven energy generation by evaporation and moisture have been widely investigated. Especially, it has been regarded as one of most promising technologies to reliably convert the water energy into electric energy. MoS2-based composites (MoS2-airlaid paper and uneven MoS2-SA) were placed in a water environment for quantitatively measuring the performance of water-driven energy generation (Fig. 6 a). Under humid conditions, the airlaid paper can only provide a voltage of ~ 30 mV, while MoS2-airlaid paper and uneven MoS2-SA can provide stable open-circuit voltage output up to ~ 200 mV (Fig. 6b, S8 and S9). Base on previous experiments and reports, a possible mechanism of water-driven energy generation was outlined in Fig. 7 . Hydrogen atoms could form covalent bonds with sulfur during the fabrication process of MoS2.[18] Under humid conditions, these hydrogen ions are easily dissociated from the MoS2 surface to free state hydronium ions. With uneven distribution of MoS2, more hydrogen ions are dissociated from the high-content side to free state hydronium ions, forming a concentration gradient of hydronium ions, which leads to a potential difference. The water-driven energy generation of MoS2-airlaid paper was measured multiple times on different dates and different solutions to verify its stability. As shown in Figure S10 and S11, the output voltage can be maintained between 150 ~ 300 mV, which is relatively stable under ignoring the influence of other factors. The output voltage did not increase significantly after adding carbon dots (Fig. 8 a). The current is tested after stabilization (Fig. 8b), and the power output can reach ~ 0.9 mW m−2 with current of 2.7 µA. After adding CDs, the current does not change significantly, which shows CDs have almost no effect on energy generation. Through the above results, the MoS2-based composites are capable of full-time functions by solar water evaporation and water-driven energy generation. The output voltage of MoS2-based composites can be further scaled up to meet application requirements by connecting several devices in series. As shown in Fig. 8c, the voltage can be increased to ~ 400 mV for two devices (MoS2-airlaid paper) (Figure S12 and Fig. 8c), ~600 mV for three devices, ~800 mV for four devices, respectively, revealing a linear increase behavior. The result suggests that MoS2-based composites possess the outstanding scaling properties.

In addition to the energy generation in the water environment, the evaporation-based energy generation is also regarded as one of most promising approaches to reliably convert the water energy into electrical energy.[48] In this regard, the prepared MoS2-based composites are capable of producing steam and concentrating energy at the same time under sunlight (Fig. 9 a). Under one sun, the voltage output of MoS2-based composites can reach ~ 300 mV, which is ~ 100 mV higher than that of under ambient conditions on the same day. Similarly, the voltage of two devices connected in series can reach ~ 540 mV under one sun, but ~ 400 mV under ambient conditions (Fig. 9b). From the Fig. 9c and d, we can observe that the voltage output drops when the light off. Similarly, a stable voltage output is maintained under ambient conditions, while once the light on, the voltage output will rise. These results prove that evaporation can promote the generation of electric energy. It may be due to the rapid water evaporation contributing to the high-performance energy conversion in the micro pores through the capillary action. The current can rise to 6.7 µA after half an hour of one sun illumination, once the light off, the current will gradually decrease (Fig. 9e). The long-term water-driven energy generation was measured to examine the reliability of developed energy generator. As shown in Fig. 9f, the long-term energy output was stabilized at ~ 400 mV over 5 h through two devices in series under ambient conditions, showing the continuous electricity generation of MoS2-based composites. Also, under solar radiation, MoS2-based composites can simultaneously show the performance of solar evaporation and energy generation (Fig. 9g). The MoS2-airlaid paper shows an evaporation rate of 1.2 kg m-2h−1 while also generating a voltage output of ~ 300 mV under one sun. While maintaining the same evaporation rate, electrical output of ~ 540 mV can be generated with two devices. Next, we attempt to store the generated energy using a capacitor with 100 µF and ~ 250 mV can be charged within 100 s by one device (Fig. 9h and i). Although the evaporation rate of MoS2@CDs-airlaid paper can be increased to 1.4 kg m-2h−1, the voltage output has not been significantly improved. Similarly, the voltage output can also be up to ~ 300 mV (Fig. 10 a), and after half an hour of one sun illumination, the current can rise to 6.8 µA (Fig. 10b). The above results show that MoS2-based composites have ability to achieve full-time work by dual functions: i) Solar evaporation and enhanced energy generation under the sun; ii) Energy generation on cloudy days or at night. In addition, the output voltage of MoS2-based composites can be scaled up and stored to meet application requirements by connecting several devices in series.

In summary, we focus our research on the maximum utilization of resources through solar evaporation and water-driven energy generation strategies. We develop in this work MoS2-based porous system as solar evaporators for enhanced steam generation by carbon dots (CDs) regularizing light utilization. A high evaporation rate of up to 1.9 kg m−2h−1 can be achieved with the full-spectrum responsive under one sun. Additionally, the developed MoS2-SA and MoS2-airlaid paper generators have provided a facile solution to generate energy in a water environment. The power output of ~ 0.9 mW m−2 with a voltage of ~ 200 mV can be readily derived under ambient conditions. Once placed under one sun, the power output can rise to ~ 3.35 mW m−2 with voltage of ~ 300 mV synchronously with efficient solar evaporation. Such fabricated MoS2-based composites can also be scaled up, with improved performance proportional to the number of MoS2-based energy generators used. The voltage can be increased to ~ 400 mV for two devices, ~600 mV for three devices, ~800 mV for four devices under ambient conditions, and while maintains a high evaporation rate, electrical energy output of ~ 540 mV can be generated with two devices under one sun. In addition, the generated energy can be stored using capacitors for sustained utilization. Significantly, the work demonstrates the MoS2-based composites are capable of full-time work only relying on solar energy and water. And it builds up a sustainable solution to overcome the energy deficiency in the world, by utilizing solar evaporation and water-driven energy generation technologies, which can be scalable, renewable, and practical with maximum utilization of resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Key R&D Program of China (Grant 2016YFA0200200) and Wuhan Science and Technology Bureau of China (2018010401011280).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.131008.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
